content.content: From Proto-Italic *fāmā, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéh₂meh₂, from *bʰeh₂- (“to speak”). Cognate to Ancient Greek φήμη (phḗmē, “talk”).
hascine propter rēs maledicās fāmās ferunt.
Is it on account of these things that they spread slanderous reports?
“Oenotrian men tilled [the land]; now rumor [has it that their] descendants call the nation ‘Italy’ after the name of its leader, [Italus].”
Fāma tamen clāra est; et adhūc sine crīmine vīxī.
My good name is nevertheless unstained; and so far I have lived without blame.
43 BCE – c. 17 CE, Ovidius, Heroides 17.17, (translation Benham's Book of Quotations 1948)
Many fear their reputation, few their conscience.
61 CE – c. 112 CE, Plinius Minor, Epistulae 3.20.9
legō (present infinitive legere, perfect active lēgī, supine lēctum); third conjugation

Etymology tree
Proto-Indo-European *leǵ-
Proto-Indo-European *-eti
Proto-Indo-European *léǵeti
Proto-Italic *legōder.
Latin legō
From Proto-Italic *legō, from Proto-Indo-European *léǵeti, from *leǵ-. Cognates include Ancient Greek λέγω (légō, “to speak, to choose, to mean”) and Albanian mbledh. May be related to lēx.
For sense 6 (“to read”), compare typologically разбира́ть (razbirátʹ), разбира́ться (razbirátʹsja) (< брать (bratʹ)), разбо́рчивый (razbórčivyj) (по́черк (póčerk)).

content: to collect, gather, bring together, catch

content.content: “… Date volnera lymphīs / abluam, et, extrēmus sī quis super hālitus errat, / ōre legam.” …
“Grant that I may wash her wounds with water, and, if any last breath wanders over her, I will gather it with my lips.”
[…] dum ne quem militem legeret ex eo numero quibus senatus missionem reditumque in patriam negasset ante belli finem.
[…] provided he did not choose any soldier from those to whom the Senate had refused discharge and a return home before the end of the war
27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita 26.1

content: to take out, pick out, extract, remove
to take to one's self unjustly, carry off, steal, purloin, plunder, abstract

content.content: līlium n (genitive līliī or līlī); second declension
Borrowed from Ancient Greek λείριον (leírion). See there for more.

From Proto-Italic *ū̆skʷe, from Proto-Indo-European *úds-kʷe, from *úd-s (“out, outward”, genitive) + *-kʷe (“and”). Cognate with Sanskrit उच्चा (uccā́), Younger Avestan 𐬎𐬯𐬗𐬀 (usca, “up, out”), Russian вы- (vy-, “out from”), Proto-Germanic *ūt, English out.
However, cf. Lewis & Short, which says: usquĕ, adv. [us- for ubs-, from ubi with locative s; and que for qued, old abl. of quis; v. Corss. Ausspr. 2, 471; 838; cf.: quisque, usquam].

content: as far as, up to, right until, all the way

content.content: Eōsdem facile addūcam, ut tē haec quae vastāre iam prīdem studēs relinquentem, usque ad portās prōsequantur.
I shall easily persuade the same [men], that as you are leaving these [things] which you have long been eager to destroy, they may escort [you] all the way to the [city] gates.
63 BCE, Cicero, Catiline Orations Oratio in Catilinam Prima in Senatu Habita.21

from the beginning to the end
(literally, “from the egg to the apples”)

, Latin Vulgate translation of Psalm 71:8; Canadian national motto
Ā marī usque ad mare.
rēctus (feminine rēcta, neuter rēctum, comparative rēctior, superlative rēctissimus, adverb rēctē); first/second-declension participle

Perfect passive participle of regō (“to keep or lead straight, to guide”). Inherited from Proto-Italic *rektos, corresponding to Proto-Indo-European *h₃reǵtós (“having moved in a straight line”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₃reǵ- (“to straighten, direct”).

content: led straight along, drawn in a straight line, straight, upright.

content.content: Quae rectis lineis suos ordines servant
Which preserve their order in straight lines
c. 52 BCE, Julius Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Gallico VII.23

content: morally right, correct, lawful, just, virtuous, noble, good, proper, honest.

content.content: Via stultī rēcta in oculīs eius; quī autem sapiēns est audit cōnsilia.
The way of a fool is right in his own eyes: but he that is wise hearkeneth unto counsels. (Douay-Rheims trans., Challoner rev.: 1752 CE)
c. 347 CE – 420 CE, Hieronymus, Vulgate Proverbs.12.15
domus f (genitive domūs or domī); irregular, variously declined, fourth declension, second declension

For Proto-Italic *domos, from Proto-Indo-European *dṓm (“house, home”), from root *dem- (“to build”). Cognates include Ancient Greek δόμος (dómos), Albanian dhomë (“a chamber, a room”), Avestan 𐬛𐬀𐬨- (dam-) Sanskrit दम (dáma), Proto-Slavic *domъ and further to English timber. At least indirectly cognate to Latin dominus.
The feminine gender is probably due to the original root noun; attempts to transfer it to the 4th declension are due to 2nd declension feminines being unusual outside of tree names. Some manuscripts of Plautus show forms in dem-; De Vaan (2008) doubts their authenticity.

content: house, home (the building where a person lives)
Let the good fortune of the

content.content: nox erat et tōta lūmina nūlla domō
It was night, and [there were] no lights in the whole house.

content: any dwelling-place or abode (of people or animals)
the place of one's birth or residence, native country, town
household, family (the dependants of the head of a house)
a group of disciples, school; an intellectual movement

content.content: ut non quietior populus domi esset quam militiae
so that the people should not become lazier in the time of peace than that of war
27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita 1.38
cōgitō (present infinitive cōgitāre, perfect active cōgitāvī, supine cōgitātum); first conjugation

content: quod non ego non modo audiam sed etiam videam planeque sentiam.
You do nothing, you plan nothing, you
of nothing which I not only do not hear, but which I do not see and know every particular of.
to ponder, meditate, reflect, consider (i.e. think of, about, over)
to intend, design, purpose, plan, devise (i.e. have in mind)

content.content:
From earlier en, from Proto-Italic *en, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁én (“in”). Cognates include English in, Ancient Greek ἐν (en).
The ablative is from the locative, and the accusative is from the directional.
omne adeo genvs in terris hominvmqve ferarvmqve et genvs æqvorevm pecvdes pictæqve volvcres in fvrias ignemqve rvvnt
So far does every species on earth of man and beast, whether the aquatic species, livestock, or painted-winged, collapse into the frenzies and the fire
c. 37 BCE – 30 BCE, Vergilius, Georgicon III
Poison is drunk from a gold cup
Et Verbum caro factum est, et habitavit in nobis :
And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us,
Et ingressus angelus ad eam dixit : Ave gratia plena : Dominus tecum : benedicta tu in mulieribus.
And the angel being come in, said unto her: Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women.
De introductione religionis Christianæ in Islandiam.
On the introduction of Christianity to Iceland.
27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita I.6
The Romans marched against their enemy
27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita I.12
and being distributed in tribes according to their respective nations
c. 52 BCE, Julius Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Gallico VII.19
